# What the Architect Owns {#sec-what-architect-owns}

::: {.epigraph}
> *"The architect's two most important tools are the eraser at the drafting board and the wrecking bar at the site."*
>
> — Frank Lloyd Wright, *New York Times* (1953)
:::

::: {.column-margin}
**Author's Note:** Frank Lloyd Wright, the iconic American pioneer of organic architecture, recognized that the power to destroy and revise is just as important as the power to create. For us, this summarizes the conclusion of the book. AI-assisted loops may generate candidates and automate routine checks, but accountable people or organizational roles must define what may advance, what evidence is sufficient, when to escalate or waive a rule, and who answers for a consequential commitment.
:::

```{=latex}
\abstract*{Architecture 2.0 changes the architect's work, but it does not remove accountable decision rights from the loop. This chapter returns to the opening moonshot and asks what must remain assigned when AI-assisted systems, models, tools, and environments can participate in architecture work. Checks and routine transitions may be automated or delegated; accountable people or organizational roles define scope, authority, evidence sufficiency, escalation, waivers, and consequential commitment, and the field must build shared infrastructure that makes those assignments reviewable.}
```

::: {.callout-crux}
When automated participants, tools, and architecture environments can act inside the loop, what
decision rights must remain visibly assigned, and what must the field share so
those assignments can be compared?
:::

To answer those questions, consider the lighthouse prompt that opened the book. It
asked for a low-power, 64-bit RISC-V-based compute subsystem for a real-time mobile
XR workload. At the start of
the book, that prompt was a provocation.
It looked like a request for a future hardware foundation model[^fn-arch-hardware-foundation-model].
At this point, it should read differently. The prompt is not powerful because it is
short. It is powerful because it exposes a missing design loop.

[^fn-arch-hardware-foundation-model]: The phrase names a tempting but wrong reading of the lighthouse prompt, a single broadly trained hardware model that maps intent to designs. This chapter reframes that desire as a missing loop problem, where representation, tools, evidence, rejection, and ownership must be present before any result can be trusted.

That loop includes workload definition, architecture representation, tool
interfaces, compound method roles, feedback budgets, supporting evidence records,
rejection authority, and accountable decisions. It also exposes the central conclusion
of Architecture 2.0. AI systems do not eliminate the computer architect. They
change what the architect must understand, delegate, and govern.

## Accountable Decision Rights Bound the Loop

The architect's responsibility moves upward, but not every check has to move
through one person's hands. Simulators, policy checks, regression suites, and
declared gates may reject candidates or advance routine transitions
automatically. The accountable architect or organizational role defines the
scope of that automation, the authority each check carries, the evidence needed
for each class of decision, the escalation and waiver paths, and the point at
which a consequential commitment requires explicit acceptance. The work can be
distributed; answerability cannot disappear into the distribution.

In this chapter, *the architect* therefore names the accountable decision role,
not necessarily one individual performing every operation. Large programs may
assign workload scope, software compatibility, physical signoff, security, and
product acceptance to different authorized roles. The loop remains governed
when those rights, handoffs, and limits are visible and each consequential
decision has an answerable owner.

That responsibility is especially sharp near silicon commitment. A generative
model or an automated optimizer is not answerable for a tapeout decision. Some software
systems are also hard to roll back, but tapeout makes the commitment unusually
durable. A hardware bug ships in silicon, where a respin can cost millions of
dollars and months of schedule. A named tapeout or product role therefore has
to own the acceptance criteria, unresolved waivers, and commitment explicitly.
The job shifts from crafting every artifact by hand to defining and reviewing
the evidence path that justifies commitment while remaining answerable for the
artifact's consequences.

::: {.callout-learning-objectives}
After this chapter you can draw the architect-owned commitment boundary for a
loop. That means you can:

- **Distinguish** routine checks and transitions that may be automated from decision rights that must remain assigned and answerable;
- **Decide** which state, evidence, authority, workload records, escalation paths, and waivers a loop must expose before delegation is credible;
- **Explain** why architects must understand the loop machinery without personally executing every step;
- **Answer** the strongest objections to Architecture 2.0 without treating automation as ownership;
- **Identify** the field-building stack needed for comparable, evidence-bearing loops;
- **State** why every field of the design-loop card ultimately resolves to an accountable person or organizational role.
:::

## Return to the Moonshot

To see how these shifted responsibilities manifest in practice, we must return to the lighthouse prompt that provoked this inquiry in the first place:

> Design a low-power, 64-bit RISC-V-based compute subsystem for an
> XRBench-class real-time mobile XR workload. Realize it as a vector-capable
> CPU, accelerator, or SoC block under a 3\ W TDP target in a 3\ nm-class LP
> mobile process, and return a design-space report with evidence and rejected
> alternatives.

This prompt may seem straightforward, but beneath the surface it binds together
workload, software, architecture, and physical constraints. For an AI-assisted
loop to act on this prompt, these implicit contracts must become explicit
constraints and rejection criteria.

::: {.callout-lighthouse title="The prompt hides interface contracts"}
**Context.** The opening request is not a single hardware specification. It
bundles workload, software, architecture, technology, and evidence obligations
that must be made explicit before an automated participant can act on them.

**In the Lighthouse prompt.** "XRBench-class real-time mobile XR workload"
creates an obligation to specify a workload distribution, quality-of-experience
target, benchmark version, traces, and software stack. For an automated system, this workload
obligation has to be represented as machine-readable data: scenario labels, trace
provenance, coverage limits, excluded cases, quality-of-experience labels, and
out-of-distribution (data or inputs that differ significantly from the examples a model was trained on, often degrading performance) rejection conditions. "64-bit RISC-V-based" means the AI loop
needs access to correct compiler toolchains, simulators, and instruction semantics,
rather than just generating text that looks like RISC-V. The fragment "vector-capable CPU,
accelerator, or SoC (System-on-Chip) block" defines the system's allowed action space. The loop
must be given explicit rules about which integration paths it can explore and
which interfaces are fixed.

**Evidence boundary.** "3\ W TDP target" and "3\ nm-class LP mobile process"
create power, thermal, and physical-design constraints; an optimizer evaluating
candidates must not confuse a fast proxy average-power estimate with the sustained
thermal or signoff evidence required for a final commitment.

**Takeaway.** The accountable decision role determines which contracts are
fixed, which are exposed as actions to the automated tool, which checks may
advance candidates automatically, and what evidence is sufficient for a
consequential commitment.
:::

This need for explicit evidence introduces a hierarchy of commitment. As an AI-assisted design loop explores candidates, it can draw on different levels of fidelity, from fast, imprecise proxies to cycle-accurate simulation, Register Transfer Level (RTL) verification, emulation, synthesis, place-and-route, and signoff checks. The accountable role defines which evidence is required for each proposed decision and may delegate that mapping through policy and automated gates. A strong check can inform an exploratory study, and no amount of fidelity silently authorizes an irreversible action. Decision consequences form the commitment ladder, while evidence sources remain a separate axis mapped to each proposed decision (@fig-commitment-ladder).

![**Evidence and commitment are separate:** Four representative evidence-source families do not align one to one with the five commitment levels. Evidence differs in fidelity, cost, scope, and validation, while decisions differ in consequence and reversibility. The accountable decision role maps the evidence requirement and rejection condition for each decision; moving to a stronger evidence source does not authorize a stronger commitment by itself.](images/F10-commitment-ladder){#fig-commitment-ladder width="100%" fig-alt="Two-column diagram separating four representative evidence-source families from five commitment levels. Both feed an accountable mapping that defines the evidence and rejection condition required for a proposed decision."}

The requested deliverable also matters. A design-space report is different
from RTL. RTL is different from a verified implementation. A verified
implementation is completely different from a physically routable, timing-closed tapeout. (Logical correctness in RTL does not by itself establish whether power delivery, thermal bounds, and wire routing can physically close through rigorous signoff tools.) Each step changes the
evidence standard. The same prompt can support a brainstorming loop, a
research prototype, a simulator-backed design-space exploration, or a
high-commitment implementation loop. Treating all of those deliverables as
the same is one of the fastest ways to overclaim.

To avoid this overclaiming, the framework developed here turns the prompt into a
set of explicit questions:
What task is being solved? What representation is available? What world model
does the loop assume? What tools can the system act on? What method roles are
allowed? What feedback is affordable? What evidence would make the result
credible? What alternatives were rejected? Who can stop the loop? Who is
accountable for the decision, including any automated transition or waiver?

Together, those questions define the part of
the design space the architect exposes to methods, tools, and AI-assisted systems. Choosing
what to expose is an architectural act. Expose too little and the loop cannot
find useful alternatives. Expose too much without constraints, evidence, and
rejection authority, and the loop can optimize the wrong object or cross a
commitment boundary before anyone understands what happened.

::: {.callout-architect-checkpoint title="The Exposure Gate"}
When defining an AI-loop's environment, check the boundary of exposed actions. Has an accountable role provided explicit constraints, evidence checks, and stop conditions for every knob the automated optimizer can turn? If not, it may optimize an invalid metric and cross a commitment boundary unnoticed. For example, if L2 cache size is exposed as a tunable action, a static area constraint or routing-congestion checker can reject physically unroutable floorplans automatically under a declared rule.
:::

Enforcing those boundaries marks the shift from prompt to loop. The prompt
motivates the work. The loop makes the work inspectable.

The phrase *prompt-to-loop* is deliberately more durable than prompt-to-chip.
Prompt-to-chip asks whether a system can produce an impressive final artifact
from a sentence. Prompt-to-loop asks whether it can preserve the task, state,
tools, evidence, rejected alternatives, and accountable decision boundary that make the
next architectural commitment believable. The second question remains important
even after today's models and tools are obsolete.

## Nondelegable Decision Rights

Even the most explicit loop leaves decision rights that cannot be orphaned.
Most execution can be delegated: models can propose, simulators can measure,
formal tools can reject, policy engines can route routine transitions, and
specialists can review their domains. What must remain assigned is authority to
set the task and scope, approve the automation envelope, decide what evidence
is sufficient, define escalation and waiver paths, and accept consequential
commitments. These rights may be distributed across authorized organizational
roles, but they cannot be transferred to a model as if the model could answer
for the outcome.

::: {.callout-architect-checkpoint title="The Multi-Agent Authority Gate"}
When splitting an architecture task across multiple automated participants,
trace each decision right. Which system or tool may halt or advance a routine
transition? Which observation requires escalation, who may grant a waiver, and
which accountable role accepts the consequential commitment? Adding more
participants must not make those assignments disappear.
:::

Other high-stakes fields have already learned, at real cost, where that authority has to sit.

::: {.callout-field-note title="The reviewers who could say no, too late"}
In clinical genomics, published predictors claimed to match cancer patients to the chemotherapy they would respond to, and were used to enroll real patients in trials. Two outside statisticians who tried to reproduce the analysis found it built on mislabeled, shifted data, and the predictors were worthless [@BaggerlyCoombes2009]. The authority to reject on that evidence existed, but it sat outside the loop, with reviewers never given a seat before commitment. The objection arrived only after 117 patients had been enrolled.

**Lesson:** When a loop delegates work across tools, authority to halt on independent evidence must be assigned before the commitment, not discovered after the tapeout.
:::

Understanding this machinery is not the same as personally executing every
step. An architect need not write every harness, inspect every timing path, or
run every place-and-route job. The architect does need enough understanding of
the abstractions, tool contracts, evidence limits, and likely failure modes to
set valid acceptance criteria, challenge anomalous results, judge a waiver, and
know when a specialist or stronger check is required. A role that cannot explain
what an automated check establishes, what it misses, and why it may advance a
claim cannot responsibly delegate authority to that check.

This makes the accountable work explicitly full-stack. A future architect may
not be only a CPU architect, accelerator architect, compiler specialist, or
physical-design expert. Specialists and automated systems can operate pieces of
the stack; accountable roles compose their decisions by determining which
hardware knobs may change, which software contracts remain fixed, which
workload scenarios matter, which checks carry authority, and which deployment
consequences are acceptable.

This division separates delegated loop work from accountable decision rights
(@fig-architect-owned-boundary). Automated methods may represent state,
generate candidates, evaluate proxies, call tools, critique results, summarize
evidence, preserve provenance, and reject candidates under declared rules.
Those are substantial contributions. They do not set their own scope or
authority, decide whether their evidence is sufficient for a new consequence,
grant themselves waivers, or become answerable for product risk. An automated
optimizer might tune a core's thermal profile, for instance, while a package or
product role remains accountable for the rule that blocks a reliability or
certification violation and for any waiver that permits the design to advance.

![**Accountable decision rights bound the loop:** AI systems and specialists can execute many operations inside an Architecture 2.0 loop, including declared checks and routine transitions. Scope, authority, evidence sufficiency, escalation, waivers, consequential commitment, and answerability remain visibly assigned to accountable people or organizational roles.](images/F11-architect-owned-boundary){#fig-architect-owned-boundary width="100%" fig-alt="Boundary diagram separating delegated or automated loop operations from decision rights assigned to accountable people or organizational roles."}

This distinction becomes a practical review object in
@tbl-architect-responsibilities. It is not meant to romanticize human judgment,
which can be biased, inconsistent, and incomplete. The point is to assign
decision rights and leave records that expose how technical claims connect to
organizational, economic, ethical, and deployment consequences.

| **Decision right** | **Why accountable assignment matters** | **What may be automated or delegated** | **Inspectable record** |
| --- | --- | --- | --- |
| **Intent, scope, and constraints** | The loop must serve a real objective rather than an available benchmark or proxy. | Elicit constraints, compare formulations, run policy checks, and reject declared violations. | Problem statement, non-goals, workload slice, hard constraints, and accountable role. |
| **Abstraction and representation** | Encoded state determines what the loop can see, optimize, ignore, or simplify incorrectly. | Translate artifacts, organize traces, find gaps, and propose structured schemas. | Architecture schema, exposed design space, assumptions, hidden-state list, and redaction boundary. |
| **Evidence sufficiency** | Evidence must match the consequence, reversibility, and cost of being wrong. | Gather measurements, track provenance, estimate uncertainty, replay baselines, and route anomalies. | Tool and environment contracts, calibration records, evidence summary, validity scope, and escalation threshold. |
| **Authority, escalation, and waivers** | A loop needs declared rules for who or what may stop, advance, reopen, or override a decision. | Apply validated gates, reject invalid candidates, advance routine transitions, and notify reviewers. | Authority matrix, automated gate configuration, exception record, waiver rationale, expiration, and reviewer. |
| **Consequential commitment and answerability** | Product, tapeout, deployment, safety, security, cost, and lifecycle consequences need an answerable owner. | Summarize tradeoffs, check completeness, monitor drift, and trigger rollback or revalidation. | Decision note, accepted and rejected alternatives, residual risks, commitment level, ownership transfer, and reopening condition. |

: **Accountable decision rights become explicit loop obligations:** Execution may be automated or delegated, while scope, authority, evidence sufficiency, escalation, waivers, consequential commitment, and answerability remain visibly assigned. {#tbl-architect-responsibilities tbl-colwidths="[18,26,27,29]"}

<span id="tbl-architect-owned-artifacts"></span>

This boundary also clarifies the word *agentic*. The book is not arguing
that every architecture loop should become autonomous. Agentic systems[^fn-ml-agentic-system] are
useful because they can act inside represented loops: call tools, maintain
state, revise plans, use feedback, and coordinate method roles. But action is
not accountability. As loops become more capable, their delegated authority and
the accountable roles around it must become more explicit.

[^fn-ml-agentic-system]: An AI system capable of pursuing complex goals over time, making its own decisions about which tools to use and what steps to take next. In this book such systems act inside represented loops; the authority assigned to their actions does not make them answerable organizational decision owners.

::: {.callout-architect-checkpoint title="The Ownership Test"}
If an AI-assisted design loop proposes an invalid artifact, the review record
must show the assignment of accountability. Can you trace which person or
organizational role defined the scope, authorized automated checks, set the
evidence threshold, approved any waiver, and accepted the consequential
commitment? If those assignments disappear, the loop has not become more
advanced; it has hidden the architecture decision.
:::

## Extracting and Codifying Tacit Knowledge

If accountable roles must define constraints and accept consequential risk,
those constraints still have to come from somewhere, and much of the time they
are not written down anywhere.

The reality of engineering in complex organizations is that the most valuable knowledge often resides not in formal specifications, but in the hard-won, tacit wisdom of domain experts [@Polanyi1966TacitDimension]. It is the packaging engineer knowing that a specific chiplet partition will silently break the power delivery mesh, the compiler lead knowing that their LLVM compiler backend cannot lower a proposed matrix layout, or a senior architect anticipating that an accelerator's coherency traffic will starve the XR display controller, integration failures that a local proxy would never catch.

A skilled human engineer naturally performs the work of hunting down this context before acting. They know who holds the undocumented knowledge, they pull it from them, and they synthesize it so that work does not stall on missing context.

An automated architecture loop can act only on represented state and allowed
interactions. Missing tacit constraints become invalid-action and proxy-mismatch
risks. The loop may stall, ask for review, or optimize a candidate that violates
packaging, compiler, coherency, or display-timing constraints.

This forces a shift in the architect's daily work. An automated participant can
help interview experts, retrieve prior decisions, and propose candidate
constraints, but an authorized domain owner or review process must validate what enters the loop.
That validation bridges the gap between undocumented organizational wisdom and
explicit loop state (@fig-tacit-extraction-bridge).

![**The Extraction Bridge:** Automated participants can help elicit and organize tacit constraints, but an authorized domain owner or review process validates what enters the loop. The validated constraints become explicit, machine-readable state such as environment contracts and rejection gates.](images/F10-tacit-extraction-bridge){#fig-tacit-extraction-bridge width="100%" fig-alt="Diagram showing domain experts, an accountable review role, and an automated participant turning undocumented constraints into explicit loop state."}

Translating hallway conversations and senior-engineer intuition into formal environment contracts and rejection gates allows bounded automation to reject bad designs *before* they waste simulation time.

An automated participant cannot treat an elicited statement as authority merely
because it can ask for one. The accountable process must resolve conflicts,
record scope and ownership, and explicitly bound the environment. In Architecture 2.0,
extracting and validating tacit knowledge is no longer just good communication.
It is a prerequisite engineering step for bounded automation.

## The Strongest Objections

This insistence on visible accountable decision rights, and on representing the
tacit constraints that bound delegated action, is a strong stance. A field-defining
claim should survive its sharpest critics, so it is worth stating the strongest
objections plainly.

The first objection is that Architecture 2.0 is just good experimental
methodology with new names. Provenance, baselines, rejection criteria, and
held-out validation[^fn-ml-held-out-validation] are not new; careful architects have always done them. The
response concedes the lineage and locates the difference. What changes is who
acts inside the loop and how fast. When a human runs the loop, tacit judgment
supplies the missing rigor. The architect remembers why a workload was excluded
or distrusts a proxy on instinct. When a method runs the loop at machine speed,
that tacit layer no longer makes the loop reviewable unless it is recorded, so
the discipline must be made explicit and machine-readable. Architecture 2.0 is
the claim that the methodology must become an engineered object, not a craft,
once automated tools participate. The book borrows
established forms deliberately. Its structured evidence record borrows from assurance cases
and Goal Structuring Notation [@Kelly2004GSN], a lineage already used in safety-critical domains such
as automotive functional safety, where a safety case must argue that a design
meets its requirements before it ships [@ISO2018Iso26262]. The fidelity ladder is
multi-fidelity modeling[^fn-ml-multi-fidelity] [@PeherstorferWillcoxGunzburger2018Multifidelity].
Naming the lineage is the point, not a weakness.

[^fn-ml-held-out-validation]: Lineage matters here. Held-out validation is one of the older rigor practices the book concedes; Architecture 2.0 asks that such practices be recorded explicitly when an automated method runs the loop.

[^fn-ml-multi-fidelity]: Here multi-fidelity modeling is the ancestor of the book's fidelity ladder, the architect-owned choice among cheap proxies and higher-cost evidence. The commitment boundary remains a separate decision about what that evidence authorizes.

The second objection is that AI for systems design is overhyped, and the most
cited result, learned chip placement, is contested. That is true, and this book
treats it as evidence rather than embarrassment. The placement dispute in
@sec-feedback-verification-trust is exactly why the field needs reproducible, end-to-end benchmarks and
shared evaluation and adjudication. An honest framework predicts that some flagship
claims will need narrowing, stronger evidence, or shared adjudication; its value
is a standard that makes the difference visible.

The third objection is that the design-loop card is process bureaucracy that
will not survive a deadline. The card is not a mandatory form; it is a
diagnostic for loops where tools or automated participants can advance candidates faster than
reviewers can reconstruct the evidence. A short initial pass can reveal whether
a result rests on a proxy nobody validated or a baseline nobody documented. The
time required and the defects found should be measured in real review settings
rather than assumed.
The cost of skipping that check is paid later, at higher commitment, where it is
most expensive. The card earns its place only when it is shorter than the
mistake it prevents.

The fourth objection is that the framework is too conservative because it
retains accountable decision rights while the goal is to deploy more capable AI
systems. Architecture 2.0 does not oppose automation. Validated checks can
reject candidates, routine transitions can advance automatically, and
specialized reviews can be delegated. The requirement is that their scope and
authority be declared, exceptions escalate, and consequential commitments
remain answerable. The design principle is not less automation. It is
evidence-matched automation with visible decision rights.

None of these objections is fatal, but each sharpens the claim. Architecture
2.0 is not the assertion that AI will design chips. It is the narrower, more
defensible claim that the design loop must become explicit enough to act on,
judge, and trust.

## What the Field Must Build

Making one team's loop explicit is a start, not a field. Architecture 2.0
becomes cumulative only when tasks, environments, evaluations, claims, and
decision rights can be compared across projects without pretending that every
artifact can be public. The required infrastructure is a stack, not another
reader-facing framework. Its layers have different maintainers and failure
modes, and progress at one layer cannot substitute for a missing layer above or
below it.[^fn-inst-ul]

[^fn-inst-ul]: Product safety faced the same infrastructure question and answered it with a neutral laboratory rather than a shared warehouse. Underwriters Laboratories works because manufacturers submit products voluntarily; a mark that survives independent testing is worth more in the market than the secrecy it costs. Shared loop infrastructure needs the same property, where contributing evidence pays better than withholding it.

@tbl-field-building-stack states the concrete build agenda. The resource
registry in @sec-appendix-c-architecture-20-resources remains the actionable
inventory of current projects, tools, datasets, venues, and missing resources;
this chapter states the properties that inventory should help the field
assemble.

| **Layer** | **What the field should build** | **What it enables** |
| --- | --- | --- |
| **Datasets and paired multi-fidelity trajectories** | Versioned tasks, workload slices, candidate actions, proxy observations, stronger-tool observations, successful and failed outcomes, costs, and redaction metadata linked across stages. | Learning when a cheap signal transfers, when it reverses, and which failures remain local to a workload, tool, or technology context. |
| **Versioned environments and tool contracts** | Maintained harnesses with legal actions, observation schemas, failure semantics, provenance, dependency versions, and explicit proprietary boundaries. | Comparable method evaluation and diagnosis when a result changes because the tool path changed. |
| **Evaluation and neutral adjudication** | Strong baselines, held-out tasks, cross-tool calibration, independent evaluation services, common challenge procedures, and documented retest or appeal paths. | Separation of method effects from private setup differences and a credible way to narrow or settle contested claims. |
| **Reporting and claim interfaces** | Design-loop cards, paper-facing claim summaries, disclosure boundaries, accepted and rejected alternatives, accountable roles, and runnable receipts when replay is claimed. | Reviewers can reconstruct the claim and distinguish context, audit, replay, and reproduction without demanding universal disclosure. |
| **Governance and maintenance** | Version ownership, integrity checks, waiver expiration, drift monitoring, ownership transfer, revalidation, retirement, and incident-driven reopening rules. | Records and evaluations remain meaningful after workloads, tools, policies, and organizations change. |
| **Incentives** | Publication and artifact credit, benchmark status, industrial value for neutral evaluation, confidentiality mechanisms, and credit for useful failure records. | Teams benefit from contributing durable evidence rather than publishing only winners or withholding every reusable lesson. |
| **Education** | Courses, labs, review exercises, and mentoring that teach task scoping, tool contracts, evidence-versus-consequence reasoning, failure analysis, redaction, and accountable delegation. | Architects learn to govern AI-assisted loops without having to personally implement every method or tool. |

: **Field building requires a connected stack:** Data, environments, evaluation, reporting, governance, incentives, and education must reinforce one another if AI-assisted architecture work is to become comparable and cumulative. {#tbl-field-building-stack tbl-colwidths="[20,42,30]"}

The stack does not require raw proprietary design disclosure. Its reporting
layer can begin with bounded records that state the task, versions, exposed
interfaces, accepted and rejected candidates, available evidence, redactions,
and accountable roles. Those records let reviewers ask not only what result was
reported, but what loop produced it and which parts of that loop are open to
inspection.

One difficult case is failure knowledge from proprietary physical-design
flows. If those records remain entirely private, outsiders cannot inspect the
evidence and future systems repeatedly encounter the same dead ends. A useful
exchange format would separate protected implementation details from a bounded
failure lesson without pretending that the public proxy reproduces the private
result.

The community could explore this mechanism through *projected proxies and
redacted traces*. When an industrial AI loop rejects a candidate because of a
proprietary physical-design constraint, the loop might map the failure to an
open or synthetic proxy that preserves the lesson without exposing the design
rule. A projected proxy could publish a relative failure when the projection
preserves the rejection condition; it should not be treated as equivalent to the
proprietary physical-design result.

```{.yaml #lst-redacted-trace include="../../../examples/design-loop-cards/redacted-level-0.yaml" lst-cap="**Redacted Rejected-Alternative Record:** A schema-valid Level 0 context card shares a rejected design path without disclosing protected physical-design rules. It bounds the public claim but does not claim replayability."}
```

This context-only pattern (@lst-redacted-trace) shows how a loop can record the
boundary of an architectural exploration while redacting absolute dimensions
and proprietary design rules. It is a valid design-loop card, not a separate
redaction schema. Its Level 0 status is deliberate. The public record names what
was attempted and what was rejected, but it does not claim that another group
can audit or replay the protected evidence. It is not a public, comparable
evidence record. Its role is diagnostic, exposing scope and omissions rather
than substituting for audit, replay, or reproduction.
The larger public view in @tbl-public-evidence-ledger names what would be needed
before treating a redacted packet as comparable evidence. Some organizations
may be unable to share that much.
The point is field-level accountability, not full disclosure. Such a record
should expose what kind of evidence exists, what was redacted, who could inspect
the protected portion, and which commitment the public record can and cannot
support.

| **Field** | **Public record** | **Why it matters** |
| --- | --- | --- |
| **Task and workload class** | Workload family, scenario, benchmark or trace version, and excluded cases. | Prevents a result from being read as broader than the task that produced it. |
| **Action-observation interface** | What the loop could change, what it could observe, and which tools or environments returned feedback. | Makes the exposed design space inspectable. |
| **Tool and model versions** | Simulator, compiler, EDA flow, benchmark, model, and prompt or policy versions when they affect the result. | Makes drift and reproduction failures diagnosable. |
| **Model and feedback budget** | Model calls, GPU-hours, energy or carbon exposure, tool or license cost, human review cost, and amortization basis when material. | Prevents AI assistance from making an expensive loop look artificially cheap. |
| **Accepted and rejected candidates** | A compact list of advanced candidates, rejected candidates, and the rule or evidence that separated them. | Makes rejection reasons available for bounded reuse. |
| **Evidence stages** | Proxy, simulation, compiler/runtime, RTL, physical, silicon, or deployment feedback used, with fidelity limits. | Shows whether the evidence matches the claimed commitment. |
| **Telemetry coverage** | Hardware stepping, software stack, deployment version, fleet cohort, sampling policy, privacy filter, canary or rollback label, incident context, and decision owner when field evidence is used. | Prevents deployment evidence from being read as cleaner or broader than it is. |
| **Redaction and ownership** | What was hidden, why it was hidden, who may inspect it, the disclosure or release boundary, and who owned the final decision. | Keeps IP, policy, and export boundaries explicit without hiding accountability. |

: **A public evidence record should expose the loop, not the proprietary design.** The minimum useful record names the task, interface, versions, candidates, evidence stages, redaction boundary, and accountable decision owner. {#tbl-public-evidence-ledger tbl-colwidths="[22,34,34]"}

::: {.callout-architect-checkpoint title="The Redaction and Comparison Gate"}
Before publishing or sharing a redacted loop record, verify its failure condition. Does it hide the workload class, action-observation interface, evidence stage, or decision owner? If any of these are missing, the record can provide context but cannot be treated as comparable evidence for an AI-mediated claim.
:::

When evaluating new benchmarks against this standard, read them by the loop fields
they expose, not by their names. ArchGym [@KrishnanEtAl2023ArchGym] and QuArch [@PrakashEtAl2025QuArch; @PrakashEtAl2025QuArchReasoning], met earlier, contribute
environment/action interfaces
and question-to-evidence review tasks. Two further examples fill fields the
book has used only lightly so far: CircuitNet [@ChaiEtAl2022CircuitNet], an open dataset of
physical-design flow records, contributes design-flow data, and ChiPBench [@WangEtAl2025ChiPBench], the
end-to-end placement benchmark that grew out of the placement dispute
(@sec-feedback-verification-trust), contributes final power-performance-area
rejection against proxy wins.
The common test is whether an AI-mediated architecture claim can show what
state it used, what it changed, what feedback it received, what failed, and who
owned the commitment.

This apparatus has a specific case in mind.

::: {.callout-war-story title="Closing the loop on the flagship"}
**The recurring stress test.** Across this book, the AlphaChip result—an AI method for chip floorplanning—has served as our concrete anchor. Its strongest claims relied on proprietary pretraining on Google's own chips, which were legitimately never released.

**The unresolvable dispute.** That missing data created a gap that could never be closed from public artifacts alone. Independent groups could not reconstruct the strongest claims, leading to an endless public debate over reproducibility.

**The path forward.** The goal of Architecture 2.0 is not to win that old argument, but to prevent the next one. A public evidence record and a redacted rejected-alternative record (@lst-redacted-trace, @tbl-public-evidence-ledger) can expose the scope and disclosure boundary of a loop without revealing proprietary IP. Those records make a claim easier to inspect, but they cannot substitute for shared evidence or independent adjudication when a stronger public commitment requires them.
:::

The missing pieces are just as important. Architecture work rarely preserves
failure records: failed runs, rejected candidates, invalid configurations,
stale benchmarks, bad proxies, tool errors, and ideas that were ruled out by
expert judgment. Yet these records are exactly what an AI-assisted design loop
needs to learn from the field's failures rather than rediscover them. A
community that records only successful artifacts gives future systems a
distorted view of architecture practice.

The goal is not to force every organization to publish internal traces. The goal
is to build schemas, examples, synthetic tasks, open benchmarks, redacted
records, and review artifacts that make credible loop design discussable.
That is how Architecture 2.0 can become a research area rather than a set of
private demos.
That goal also needs a practical incentive. Teams will preserve useful failure records
when the record pays for itself by preventing repeated high-fidelity failures,
making handoffs cheaper, supporting review credit, and showing when an old
rejection should be retired rather than repeated forever.

### Start with one loop packet

A team need not wait for the whole stack. The minimum useful target is one
credible loop packet attached to a design review, design-space exploration, or
paper. It should name the task and decision scope, wrap one tool path with a
versioned environment contract, preserve successful and failed runs, state what
evidence supports the survivor, and assign the automated and accountable
decision rights. If a second person can reconstruct what was tried, what failed,
what advanced automatically, what required review, and who remains answerable,
the packet is already useful.

The packet also carries the lifecycle boundary. A shared failure record needs
reuse limits, revalidation rules, and a retirement path so a local rejection
does not become a false universal rule. Tacit knowledge belongs only where it
can be named as state, rationale, waiver, or owner judgment. Model-side compute
and energy cost, post-silicon telemetry, integrity records, and legal, IP, or
export-control boundaries belong whenever they affect the claim. These are not
extra fields for their own sake; they determine whether the record remains valid
when tools, workloads, models, and owners change.

## Loop Invariants as Review Checks

Just as classic computer architecture relied on rigorous quantitative metrics to evaluate artifacts, Architecture 2.0 relies on the verifiable loop. A first packet is where adoption starts; the design principles are how any loop,
including that packet, gets quantitatively and qualitatively checked. Each chapter has contributed one such
principle, and together they test whether an AI-mediated loop has represented
workloads and state, bounded its actions, preserved provenance, gathered
evidence, rejected candidates, and assigned accountable decision rights. They are more useful than
a list of fashionable tools because they give reviewers, authors, and builders a
stable way to inspect new work.
Those principles, collected as review
checks, each pair with the design challenge it leaves behind
(@tbl-architecture-20-design-principles).

| **Principle** | **Where it comes from** | **Design challenge to keep in mind** |
| --- | --- | --- |
| Design the loop, not only the artifact. | @sec-moonshot | A prompt or generated artifact is not enough; the loop must expose state, action, feedback, rejection, and decision. |
| Diagnose the loop before adding AI. | @sec-design-loop-no-longer-scales | More candidates do not help if representation, action, feedback, rejection, or commitment is the actual bound. |
| State the claim as a review object. | @sec-architecture-20-ontology | A result should name workload, baseline, design space, objective, constraints, evidence, rejection rule, and decision owner. |
| Make architecture work legible. | @sec-data-representations-world-models | Data must include provenance, assumptions, costs, failed runs, and rejected alternatives, not only successful endpoints. |
| Turn tools into environments. | @sec-architecture-environments-tool-interfaces | A wrapper is credible only when it defines legal actions, observations, costs, invalid states, and rejection paths. |
| Match methods to roles. | @sec-methods-generation-prediction-optimization | A method should be chosen for the loop bottleneck it relieves, not for its reputation. |
| Escalate with commitment. | @sec-feedback-verification-trust | Evidence requirements should rise with rollback cost, blast radius, and independence of rejection authority. |
| Stop at an honest evidence level. | @sec-running-the-loop | A loop should report what its evidence supports, what it rejected, and what would overturn the decision. |
| Recognize the rejection-bound regime. | @sec-loop-patterns-across-stack | When proposals outpace trusted dispositions, improve validated rejection coverage and adjudication capacity before adding more candidates. |
| Keep accountable decision rights visible. | @sec-what-architect-owns | The record must show who or what may advance, reject, escalate, or waive, and which accountable role remains answerable for consequential commitment. |

: **Architecture 2.0 design principles are review checks:** Each principle asks whether the loop has made enough of the architecture problem explicit for another architect to inspect, reject, or extend it. Replay and reproduction require the additional artifacts appropriate to those claims. {#tbl-architecture-20-design-principles tbl-colwidths="[24,20,46]"}

These principles are deliberately phrased as checks, not slogans. A reader
reviewing a paper can ask which principle is visible and which is missing. A
researcher building a tool can ask which principle the tool makes easier to
practice. A program committee, artifact committee, or internal review group can
apply the same checks to a new paper, environment, or design-review packet.

These principles also change what counts as a mature research contribution. The
loop is becoming part of the contribution. When the architect's work moves
toward setting evidence standards, authorizing automated checks, and assigning
consequential decisions, papers should make those obligations inspectable
rather than treating them as process details outside the contribution.

## Beyond the Current Loop

Mastering those review skills is what carries an architect past the current loop,
and the horizon beyond this book is not another autonomy level. It is the moment
when loops begin to improve their own representations, propose new tasks,
repair tool interfaces, organize failure records, and recalibrate evidence
standards. That possibility is powerful, but it does not change the core
obligation. A loop that can adapt can also adapt toward benchmark gaming,
hidden failures, tool overfitting, or biased traces.

This matters most because the design target no longer holds still. When software
is continuously regenerated and retrained, as @sec-design-loop-no-longer-scales
argued and @sec-loop-patterns-across-stack showed at fleet scale, a specific
artifact ages as the workload it served moves on. The artifact remains the thing
committed at a point in time; the loop is the durable discipline for deciding
whether that commitment is still supported. What remains stable is how intent
is bounded, evidence is demanded, candidates are rejected, and commitment is
decided as the target moves.

The durable way to read emerging design assistants is therefore not by their
feature lists, which will change, but by the partition of design autonomy:
which transitions the loop may make within stated bounds, which checks or
specialists hold delegated authority, and which consequential decision rights
remain assigned to accountable people or organizational roles
[@ReddiYazdanbakhsh2025Architecture20]. That partition, not the autonomy label,
is what the architect must keep designing and maintaining.

## The Architect's Standing Obligation

Designing that partition does not require a new instrument, because the
operational checklist already exists. The trust checklist in
@sec-feedback-verification-trust and the design-loop card and rubric in
@sec-appendix-b-design-loop-card provide that checklist for a single claim and
for a whole project, and this chapter does not reprint them. It adds the
assignment question. Each consequential card field must resolve to a decision
right held by an accountable person or organizational role. The same person
need not hold every right, and routine checks need not wait for manual approval.
The record must show the authorized automation envelope, evidence threshold,
escalation and waiver path, consequential acceptance, and lifecycle transfer.

That bar is modest but durable. Make the loop visible enough to critique and
compare, then make its decision rights visible enough to know who or what may
act and who remains answerable. Replay or reproduction additionally requires
the runnable inputs, environment, provenance, and receipts appropriate to the
claim.

::: {#pri-human-accountable .callout-design-principle title="Keep accountable decision rights visible"}
Checks and routine transitions may be automated or delegated. The loop must
still show who or what may advance, reject, escalate, or waive; which accountable
person or organizational role accepts consequential commitment; and who must
explain and revise the decision when the evidence or context changes.
:::

::: {.callout-architect-checkpoint title="Standing Obligation"}
- **Assignment test:** Can a reviewer identify the role that set scope, authorized automated checks, judged evidence sufficiency, and accepted consequential commitment?
- **Exception test:** Are escalation, waiver, rollback, and reopening paths explicit before an exception occurs?
- **Lifecycle test:** When tools, workloads, or owners change, does the record transfer or retire those decision rights rather than silently preserving stale authority?
:::

## Final Thoughts

When this book opened with the lighthouse prompt, asking a generative method to
generate an entire XR subsystem from a text description, it may have sounded
like a prediction that the human architect's job was ending. The framework
developed across these chapters argues the exact opposite.

The tools, models, and automated systems driving this shift will keep changing,
so any catalog of today's capabilities will quickly become obsolete. The one
durable anchor is engineering discipline. We are crossing the threshold from *artifact
scarcity* to *commitment scarcity*. When generating a plausible accelerator or
floorplan becomes fast and cheap, the bottleneck shifts to the loop that
produced it. A workflow becomes rejection-bound when proposal arrival exceeds
its trusted disposition capacity. Progress then depends on how much unsupported
work can be rejected, escalated, or bounded before it hardens into a commitment.

The book's two running examples were never separate arguments. The lighthouse
prompt showed the architecture we could have, a hard problem carried by a loop a
team can govern through visible decision rights. The AlphaChip war story showed the architecture we have,
a real and celebrated result the field could not fully replay or adjudicate at
the strongest public commitment level, not because the method was shown wrong but
because the shared standard that could settle it was never built. Set side by
side, they mark the distance this book set out to close. The moonshot names the
reach and the war story names the debt. The bridge between them is the discipline
these chapters describe, the explicit, evidence-bearing, redactable loop that
lets an ambitious result become a claim the field can inspect, delimit, and
adjudicate. Replay or reproduction follows only when the corresponding packet is
available. That bridge is not a capability anyone waits to be handed.
It is infrastructure the community builds.

Architecture 2.0 is not about trusting machines more. It is about building loops
that earn our trust. The AI-assisted system can generate, predict, and optimize,
and declared checks can reject or advance routine transitions. The system is not
answerable for the organizational consequences. Accountable people or roles
must set its authority and accept consequential risk through the appropriate
review and commitment process. The task ahead is not to wait for an AI that can design a computer
from a single sentence. It is to enforce the engineering discipline, the
evidence standards, and the rejection boundaries that make those AI-assisted
claims credible. Designing the loop is now part of designing the architecture,
alongside the artifact it produces, never in place of it.
